# SQLite conformance inventory

- Inventory version: `1.3.0`
- Coordination issue: [#190](https://github.com/lukevanin/swiftql/issues/190)

This report is generated deterministically from [`SQLiteConformanceInventory.json`](../../Tests/SwiftQLSQLiteConformanceFixtures/SQLiteConformanceInventory.json).

## Claim and limits

Evidence-backed conformance for SwiftQL's existing public SQLite syntax and adopted behavior surface; not complete SQLite grammar coverage.

- SQLite's reviewed syntax documentation is a dimension checklist, not an executable grammar.
- Only public SwiftQL constructors named by feature records are in scope.
- Supported syntax requires deterministic rendering and successful preparation by a version-identified real SQLite engine.
- Representative execution proves selected semantics; it does not enumerate every legal SQLite interaction.
- Planned suites and deferred features carry no invented evidence and are not counted as supported.

A feature counts as supported only when it references explicit real-SQLite `prepare` evidence. Supported syntax also requires renderer evidence. Capability-gated and intentionally unsupported records are listed but never included in the supported count.

## Gates, deviations, and deferrals

| Feature | Required capabilities | Schema requirements | Deviations or scope reason | Deferral |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `northwind.compound.customer-supplier-cities` | `compound-select`, `database-pool-driver`, `sqlite-core-parser` | Customers and Suppliers expose compatible city, company, contact, and relationship columns with deterministic compound ordering. | — | — |
| `syntax.compound.current-set-operators` | `compound-select`, `sqlite-core-parser` | Both compound branches expose compatible typed row layouts. | — | — |
| `syntax.compound.direct-scalar-gap` | `compound-select`, `sqlite-core-parser` | A stable one-column scalar row layout and alias are required across every branch. | Current compound APIs rebuild row metadata through XLResult and require a wrapper for scalar rows. | [#43](https://github.com/lukevanin/swiftql/issues/43) for `v1.4`: Issue #43 owns direct scalar compound and CTE row support. |
| `syntax.compound.intersect-except-prepare-gap` | `compound-select`, `sqlite-core-parser` | Both compound branches expose compatible typed row layouts. | — | — |
| `northwind.cte.order-subtotals` | `database-pool-driver`, `named-bindings`, `sqlite-core-parser` | Order Details supplies grouped discounted totals and a stable orderID/subtotal row shape for an ordinary CTE. | — | — |
| `syntax.cte.materialization-hints-gap` | `recursive-cte`, `sqlite-core-parser` | A typed CTE definition with an explicit materialization policy is required. | SwiftQL does not currently expose SQLite materialization hints. | [#10](https://github.com/lukevanin/swiftql/issues/10) for `v1.4`: Issue #10 owns materialized and non-materialized common tables. |
| `syntax.cte.recursive` | `recursive-cte`, `sqlite-core-parser` | Recursive branches expose a stable alias, compatible columns, and a terminating predicate. | — | — |
| `syntax.ddl.create-table` | `sqlite-core-parser` | Generated table metadata supplies names and SQLite storage declarations. | This record covers the existing create-table surface, not a complete typed SQLite DDL model. | — |
| `syntax.ddl.typed-model-gap` | `sqlite-core-parser` | Stable dialect-aware table, column, constraint, index, and schema-object metadata are required. | The existing public DDL surface is centered on generated CREATE TABLE statements. | [#139](https://github.com/lukevanin/swiftql/issues/139) for `v2`: Issue #139 owns the typed dialect-aware DDL foundation. |
| `capability.nested-transaction` | `nested-transaction-or-savepoint` | A transaction-owned table records stable logical row IDs before and after the operation. | The v1 driver has no nested transaction or savepoint hook. | [#113](https://github.com/lukevanin/swiftql/issues/113) for `v2`: Issue #113 owns the required GRDB adapter and transaction capability hooks. |
| `capability.single-connection-visibility` | `single-connection-driver` | A transaction-owned table records stable logical row IDs before and after the operation. | The v1 GRDB adapter accepts a DatabasePool, not a single-connection writer. | [#113](https://github.com/lukevanin/swiftql/issues/113) for `v2`: Issue #113 owns the required GRDB adapter and transaction capability hooks. |
| `cleanup.post-failure-reuse` | `database-pool-driver`, `transactions` | A transaction-owned table records stable logical row IDs before and after the operation. | — | — |
| `commit.empty` | `database-pool-driver`, `transactions` | A transaction-owned table records stable logical row IDs before and after the operation. | — | — |
| `commit.multiple-statements` | `database-pool-driver`, `transactions` | A transaction-owned table records stable logical row IDs before and after the operation. | — | — |
| `commit.return-value` | `database-pool-driver`, `transactions` | A transaction-owned table records stable logical row IDs before and after the operation. | — | — |

| `northwind.transaction.throwing-rollback` | `database-pool-driver`, `named-bindings`, `sqlite-core-parser`, `transactions` | A UUID-owned writable copy rolls back a thrown transaction body, preserves the original error, and remains independently readable while the canonical checksum remains unchanged. | — | — |
| `ownership.connection-pinning` | `database-pool-driver`, `transactions` | A transaction-owned table records stable logical row IDs before and after the operation. | — | — |
| `rollback.bind-failure` | `database-pool-driver`, `transactions` | A transaction-owned table records stable logical row IDs before and after the operation. | — | — |
| `rollback.body-error` | `database-pool-driver`, `transactions` | A transaction-owned table records stable logical row IDs before and after the operation. | — | — |

| `rollback.decode-failure` | `database-pool-driver`, `transactions` | A transaction-owned table records stable logical row IDs before and after the operation. | — | — |
| `rollback.driver-failure` | `database-pool-driver`, `transactions` | A transaction-owned table records stable logical row IDs before and after the operation. | — | — |

| `syntax.dml.conflict-clause` | `sqlite-core-parser` | A generated writable table supplies insert metadata. | The conflict algorithm is part of the INSERT keyword and renders as INSERT OR ROLLBACK/ABORT/FAIL/IGNORE/REPLACE INTO. | — |
| `syntax.dml.current-crud` | `sqlite-core-parser` | A generated writable table supplies insert, update, and delete metadata. | Conflict algorithms, UPSERT, and RETURNING are separate deferred sub-surfaces. | — |
| `syntax.dml.replace` | `sqlite-core-parser` | A generated writable table supplies insert metadata. | REPLACE INTO is the SQLite shorthand for INSERT OR REPLACE INTO. | — |
| `syntax.dml.returning-gap` | `sqlite-core-parser` | DML statements need typed result-row and cardinality metadata. | The current DML statement protocols are write-only and do not expose RETURNING rows. | [#57](https://github.com/lukevanin/swiftql/issues/57) for `v1.4`: Issue #57 is the first atomic RETURNING implementation; #53 and #58 cover DELETE and UPDATE. |
| `upstream.fluent.soft-delete-lifecycle` | — | Fluent model lifecycle metadata, default query scopes, and restore semantics would be required. | SwiftQL is a typed SQL construction library and does not implement ORM soft-delete lifecycle behavior.<br>Soft-delete filtering and restore semantics are ORM record-lifecycle policy, not SQLite syntax. | — |

| `syntax.expression.aggregate-functions` | `sqlite-core-parser` | Aggregate input columns use compatible numeric, comparable, or text expression types; nullable-result constructors preserve empty-input semantics. AVG accepts Int, Double, Int?, and Double? expressions and always returns Double?. TOTAL accepts the same numeric inputs, always returns Double, and preserves SQLite's 0.0 result for empty or all-NULL inputs. | Real-SQLite evidence covers every aggregate family on populated, empty, and all-NULL input, and issue #286 adds deterministic DISTINCT execution cases for COUNT, MIN, MAX, AVG, SUM, and GROUP_CONCAT.<br>Issue #141 extends AVG to integer and nullable numeric expressions. XLAggregateTests proves all four public input shapes render AVG, return Double?, ignore individual NULL values, and preserve NULL for empty and all-NULL inputs.<br>Issue #142 adds SQLite TOTAL as the explicit nonoptional alternative to SUM. XLAggregateTests proves integer, real, nullable-integer, and nullable-real inputs render TOTAL, return Double, ignore individual NULL values, and return 0.0 for empty and all-NULL inputs.<br>The deprecated SwiftQL 1.x nonoptional min, max, average, sum, and groupConcat overloads are source-compatible rendering aliases for the corresponding OrNull SQL. XLAggregateTests retains compile-time and rendering dispositions for those signatures, while the same generated real-SQLite cases execute their shared SQL semantics. | — |
| `syntax.expression.conditional-functions` | `sqlite-core-parser` | Conditional branches resolve to compatible SwiftQL result and optionality types. | The SQLite 3.32.0 minimum is set by IIF; CASE and COALESCE are available in older releases. | — |
| `syntax.expression.current-operators` | `collation:BINARY`, `collation:NOCASE`, `collation:RTRIM`, `named-bindings`, `sqlite-core-parser` | Column references, literals, and bindings use the exact compatible SwiftQL expression and optionality overloads named by this record. | Support is limited to the named and evidenced constructors; Boolean combinators, comparison ordering, remaining arithmetic, GLOB, and query-backed IN overloads are recorded separately as partial. | — |
| `syntax.expression.custom-collation` | `sqlite-core-parser` | The named collating sequence is registered on every connection the query executes against. | Built-in collations render as bare grammar tokens while a registered name renders as a quoted identifier. Both spellings resolve to the same sequence in SQLite; the split keeps issue #169's guarantee that `collate(_:)` is not an arbitrary raw-SQL escape hatch, because a caller-supplied name is escaped by the identifier formatter rather than concatenated into the keyword.<br>Registration is the application's responsibility and is not verified when the expression is built. An unregistered name fails at preparation with `no such collation sequence` rather than silently falling back to BINARY.<br>`XLCollation` changed from an enum to a `RawRepresentable` struct so a name outside the three built-ins can be expressed. Every existing spelling — `.binary`, `.nocase`, `.rtrim`, `rawValue`, `init(rawValue:)` — is preserved, but exhaustive `switch` over the former enum cases no longer compiles.<br>Equality and hashing use the collation name alone. The flag selecting bare-token versus quoted rendering is excluded, so `.nocase` and a hand-named `NOCASE` are one value: they name the same collating sequence and differ only in a spelling SQLite treats as equivalent. | — |
| `syntax.expression.date-functions` | `sqlite-core-parser` | Date inputs are ISO-8601 text expressions or constant strings accepted by SQLite's date parser. | The date, time, datetime, julianday, unixepoch, and strftime constructors take ordered XLDateModifier values covering the offset, start-of, weekday, ceiling, floor, localtime, utc, and subsecond modifiers available in SQLite 3.42.0 and later; the input-interpretation modifiers unixepoch, julianday, and auto are not modelled as named members and are reachable through XLDateModifier(_:) for callers targeting a known SQLite version.<br>Date components are exposed as integer accessors that reinterpret strftime text with CAST(... AS INTEGER); date comparison and julian-day subtraction reuse the existing generic XLComparable and floating-point operators rather than adding date-specific overloads. | — |
| `syntax.expression.json-functions` | `sqlite-json-functions` | JSON inputs are SQLite text expressions; path overloads require a valid SQLite JSON path string. | The pinned SQLite 3.51.0 environment now attests JSON_VALID/1 plus JSON_ARRAY_LENGTH/1 and /2 through PRAGMA function_list; all three public constructors prepare and execute with independent raw-SQL semantic oracles. | — |
| `syntax.expression.like-escape-gap` | `sqlite-core-parser` | A typed text expression, pattern, and single-character escape expression are required. | The escape operand is typed as a required text expression across all four optionality shapes; SQLite additionally requires it to evaluate to exactly one character, which no Swift type can express. A longer or empty value prepares successfully and fails when the statement is stepped.<br>ESCAPE renders as part of its own LIKE production rather than as a nested binary expression, so a second LIKE in the same predicate cannot absorb it. | — |
| `syntax.expression.not-in` | `sqlite-core-parser` | The right-hand side is a value list, a query returning one compatible column, or a common table exposing one column. | The negation is carried by the IN node itself rather than a wrapping NOT, so composing the predicate cannot move it outwards.<br>An empty right-hand set is total: NOT IN () is true and IN () is false for every operand, including NULL. This is the one shape where a NULL operand does not make the result unknown, and it is proven against a column that holds NULL rather than inferred.<br>The value-list, query-backed, and common-table overloads all mirror `in`, including every optional-operand shape. Issue #68 added the optional result-builder and NULL-element forms that were missing when NOT IN first shipped. | — |
| `syntax.expression.nullable-in` | `sqlite-core-parser` | The operand is an optional expression, and the candidate set is a value list, a query returning one compatible column, or a common table. | The NULL-element overloads are marked `@_disfavoredOverload`. An empty array literal carries no element type, so `in([])` matches the optional-element and non-optional-element overloads equally and is otherwise rejected as ambiguous. Disfavouring the optional-element overload makes the non-optional one win that tie while a list containing NULL still selects the optional one.<br>The result of every optional-operand overload is `Optional<Bool>`: a NULL operand, or an exhausted search over a set containing NULL, is unknown rather than false. | — |
| `syntax.expression.numeric-comparable-functions` | `compile-option:ENABLE_MATH_FUNCTIONS`, `sqlite-core-parser` | Arguments satisfy the public numeric or comparable SwiftQL expression constraints. | ABS, both zero-place ROUND overloads, ROUND(to:), FLOOR, scalar MIN, and scalar MAX are typechecked, rendered, prepared, and executed; the optional ROUND case explicitly proves NULL propagation. | — |
| `syntax.expression.operator-prepare-gap` | `named-bindings`, `sqlite-core-parser` | Operands satisfy the public SwiftQL numeric, Boolean, comparable, text, and optional type constraints. | The generated suite adds prepared and executed arithmetic-precedence and GLOB cases to the existing renderer checks.<br>Issue #287 part one proves every public Boolean, comparison, and equality overload with real SQLite, packed by operator family and optionality shape so each overload is one result column of a prepared statement.<br>The optional equality overloads are typed `Optional<Bool>` in Swift but render SQLite `IS` and `IS NOT`, which are total and never evaluate to NULL. The Swift result type is therefore wider than the SQL semantics.<br>Issue #287 part two proves the integer, floating-point, unary, optional-coalescing, and text overloads the same way, including the boundaries their Swift signatures cannot express.<br>The integer division and remainder overloads return a non-optional `Int`, and the floating-point division overload returns a non-optional `Double`, but SQLite yields NULL for a zero divisor in every one of them. The Swift result type is therefore narrower than the SQL, and decoding such a column into a non-optional property fails at runtime rather than at compile time.<br>SQLite `LIKE` folds case for ASCII only and `GLOB` does not fold at all; neither distinction is visible in the Swift signatures. | — |
| `syntax.expression.regexp` | `sqlite-core-parser` | A two-argument regexp function is registered on every connection the query executes against. | SQLite ships no regexp implementation, so the operator prepares only when the application has registered a two-argument regexp function. This is a schema requirement rather than a required capability: capabilities describe what the pinned SQLite build provides, and no build provides regexp. It is also not a deferral, because nothing in SwiftQL is blocking. An unregistered statement fails with `no such function: regexp`, which is asserted rather than only documented.<br>SQLite parses `X REGEXP Y` as `regexp(Y, X)`, passing the pattern first. The registered function observes that order directly in the evidence rather than it being inferred from a match result, which a symmetric pattern would not distinguish.<br>SQLite defines no regular-expression dialect. Pattern syntax is entirely determined by the registered function, so SwiftQL makes no portability claim about patterns.<br>MATCH is not included. It is meaningful only against an FTS virtual table, which requires virtual-table DDL owned by issue #139 and an FTS5 build that is not enabled by default; it is tracked separately by issue #329. | — |
| `syntax.expression.string-functions` | `sqlite-core-parser` | PRINTF arguments are SwiftQL expressions accepted by SQLite formatting. | Both the variadic and array PRINTF constructors are typechecked, rendered, prepared, and executed with named bindings and independent semantic oracles. | — |
| `syntax.expression.type-casts` | `sqlite-core-parser` | Only the public Bool, integer, real, text, data, and optional overloads are in scope. | Boolean-to-integer conversion is represented by the stored expression without an emitted CAST; all other exposed conversions render SQLite CAST expressions.<br>The generic cast(to:) overloads and directional toInt, toDouble, toString, and toData helpers share the same explicitly constrained conversion matrix and preserve source nullability.<br>The generated matrix prepares and executes every public Bool, integer, real, text, data, and optional conversion direction; required inputs prove fixed conversion values and every optional overload proves NULL propagation. | — |
| `northwind.join.customer-order-employee-product` | `database-pool-driver`, `named-bindings`, `sqlite-core-parser` | The pinned foreign-key graph joins order 10248 through Customers, Employees, Order Details, and Products. | — | — |
| `northwind.join.left-null-manager` | `database-pool-driver`, `sqlite-core-parser` | The Employees self-reference contains a root employee whose nullable manager columns remain NULL after LEFT JOIN. | — | — |
| `syntax.join.current-inner-left-cross` | `sqlite-core-parser` | Outer-joined rows use nullable metadata and ON predicates use typed Boolean expressions. | NATURAL, USING, RIGHT, and FULL joins are excluded from this supported sub-surface. | — |
| `syntax.join.natural-using-gap` | `sqlite-core-parser` | USING needs typed shared-column identity; NATURAL JOIN needs an explicit overlap policy. | Join constraints currently use ON expressions or no constraint for CROSS JOIN. | [#45](https://github.com/lukevanin/swiftql/issues/45) for `v1.4`: Issue #45 owns NATURAL JOIN and USING. |
| `observation.cancellation.terminal` | `combine-backpressure`, `database-pool-driver`, `grdb-value-observation`, `sqlite-core-parser` | The fixture can cancel before demand, after an initial value, and while real SQLite work is in flight. | Publisher cancellation is a Combine and adapter lifecycle contract, not SQLite syntax. | — |
| `observation.commit.irrelevant-table-silent` | `database-pool-driver`, `grdb-value-observation`, `sqlite-core-parser` | The fixture contains a tracked table and an unrelated table in the same database. | — | — |
| `observation.commit.rapid-relevant` | `database-pool-driver`, `grdb-value-observation`, `sqlite-core-parser` | A single tracked row receives a deterministic series of committed values followed by a liveness write. | — | — |
| `observation.demand.incremental` | `combine-backpressure`, `database-pool-driver`, `grdb-value-observation`, `sqlite-core-parser` | A manual subscriber replenishes finite demand around committed observed-table updates, including a refresh that may already be in flight. | Finite downstream demand is a Combine publisher contract, not SQLite syntax. | — |
| `observation.demand.zero` | `combine-backpressure`, `database-pool-driver`, `grdb-value-observation`, `sqlite-core-parser` | A manual subscriber can withhold demand while a real observed table changes. | Downstream demand is a Combine publisher contract, not SQLite syntax. | — |
| `observation.isolation.independent-databases` | `database-pool-driver`, `grdb-value-observation`, `sqlite-core-parser` | Two database files expose identical schemas through distinct observed pools and an unrelated writer. | — | — |
| `observation.retry.permanent-failure` | `database-pool-driver`, `grdb-value-observation`, `sqlite-core-parser` | A typed non-null row decoder reads a real SQLite NULL to produce a permanent decoding failure. | — | — |
| `observation.retry.transient-busy` | `database-pool-driver`, `grdb-value-observation`, `sqlite-busy-result-code`, `sqlite-core-parser` | A deterministic SQLite function injects busy failures into a real observation attempt. | — | — |
| `observation.rollback.silent` | `database-pool-driver`, `grdb-value-observation`, `sqlite-core-parser`, `transactions` | A relevant write is rolled back before a later committed liveness write reaches the same observation. | — | — |
| `observation.subscription.current-initial-value` | `database-pool-driver`, `grdb-value-observation`, `sqlite-core-parser` | A stable ordered query exposes the current database state when the subscriber first requests positive demand. | — | — |
| `observation.subscription.fresh-initial-per-subscriber` | `database-pool-driver`, `grdb-value-observation`, `sqlite-core-parser` | Two subscribers observe the same ordered request before and after a committed update. | — | — |
| `observation.transaction.multi-write-coalesced` | `database-pool-driver`, `grdb-value-observation`, `sqlite-core-parser`, `transactions` | One transaction performs multiple relevant writes whose intermediate states are distinguishable from the final state. | — | — |
| `northwind.fixture.pinned-corpus` | `database-pool-driver`, `sqlite-core-parser` | The immutable northwind.db resource matches SHA-256 cb6f0071a264e150d3796f75c4b0643e32b2132e4e02370518b50a1eac3381d8, passes PRAGMA integrity_check, and exposes exactly 13 application tables, 17 views, 93 customers, 830 orders, 2,155 order-detail rows, and 77 products. | — | — |
| `northwind.key.order-details-compound` | `database-pool-driver`, `named-bindings`, `sqlite-core-parser` | Order Details declares the compound primary key (OrderID, ProductID), and order 10248 has the pinned ProductID set 11, 42, and 72. | — | — |
| `northwind.select.deterministic-pagination` | `database-pool-driver`, `sqlite-core-parser` | Products provides a deterministic ProductName then ProductID ordering for a fixed LIMIT 7 OFFSET 11 page. | — | — |
| `northwind.select.empty-result` | `database-pool-driver`, `named-bindings`, `sqlite-core-parser` | The pinned Customers table has no __SWIFTQL_MISSING__ identifier. | — | — |
| `northwind.select.nullable-shipping` | `database-pool-driver`, `named-bindings`, `sqlite-core-parser`, `sqlite-storage-classes` | Orders exposes nullable shipping metadata and a pinned unshipped order has a NULL ShippedDate. | — | — |
| `northwind.select.quoted-order-details` | `database-pool-driver`, `named-bindings`, `sqlite-core-parser` | The pinned schema contains the space-bearing table name Order Details and deterministic rows for OrderID 10248. | — | — |
| `northwind.view.packaged-decoding` | `database-pool-driver`, `named-bindings`, `sqlite-core-parser` | The pinned Order Subtotals view exposes order 10248 with subtotal 440.0. | — | — |
| `syntax.select.core` | `named-bindings`, `sqlite-core-parser` | Selected row metadata and table references belong to the same query construction scope. | This record does not claim every SQLite SELECT branch or every aggregate form. | — |
| `syntax.select.count-star-gap` | `sqlite-core-parser` | A typed all-columns expression must render as an unqualified asterisk. | all() renders an unqualified asterisk, and count(all()) counts every input row including rows whose projected values are NULL. | — |
| `syntax.select.ordering-terms` | `sqlite-core-parser` | Ordering terms wrap a typed expression in a SELECT ORDER BY clause. | — | — |
| `northwind.subquery.products-above-average` | `database-pool-driver`, `sqlite-core-parser` | Products contains deterministic prices for a scalar AVG subquery and ordered outer result. | — | — |
| `syntax.subquery.correlated-scalar` | `sqlite-core-parser` | The inner query has a typed row reader and may reference a visible outer table dependency. | The registered real-SQLite evidence covers a correlated scalar aggregate; table and IN-subquery preparation remain separate. | — |
| `syntax.subquery.nullable-shape-gap` | `sqlite-core-parser` | Nullable table references and scalar cardinality must preserve typed result metadata. | `nullableSubquery` is a distinct function rather than another `subquery` overload, because the two would differ only by return type and could not be disambiguated at a call site.<br>The pre-existing `subquery(alias:)` overload constrained to `XLMetaNullable` is deprecated rather than removed. It was unreachable: no `select` function produces a statement whose row type is a generated `Nullable` companion, so no argument satisfying it could be constructed.<br>A scalar subquery over an already-optional statement now yields one `Optional` rather than nesting a second. The `XLTypeAffinityExpression` re-labelling that previously collapsed the nested type by hand is no longer needed and has been removed from its two call sites. | — |
| `syntax.subquery.table-and-in-prepare-gap` | `sqlite-core-parser` | The subquery exposes a compatible typed row or scalar layout to the enclosing query. | Issue #288 proves both query-backed entry points with real SQLite: the result-builder and functional forms of `in(expression:)`, and `in(_ table:)` rendered as SQLite's `expr IN table-name` form, each with a non-empty and an empty inner result.<br>Optional operands of the IN overloads remain unproven and stay recorded by syntax.subquery.nullable-shape-gap. | — |

## Reproduce

```sh
python3 scripts/ci/sqlite-conformance-inventory.py check
```
